transcript.text: Hello. This video is what I think about the Philadelphia Experiment. So this is my rundown on it. I just watched, I think, a 17 or a 22 part interview of Al Belick, who was the guy that that movie Philadelphia Experiment was based off of. You can rent it. In fact, I think I shall rent it one of these days, soon, tomorrow.
If I remember. Now, the Philadelphia Experiment is, in 1942, the U.S. Army was experimenting with making ships invisible so that we could win the war. Invisible to radar.

So, I think Tesla actually invented the whole idea. And what they did, as far as I can remember from the interview, is they got these huge copper cables, copper wires, thick ones, shooting off in certain configurations

Spiraling down. Now, I think maybe even the Fibonacci sequence in the shape of a pine cone, you know, starting big and then top and going down to the bottom and making these huge, like, 20 feet high or something.

I can't remember exactly. I can't remember exactly, but they had one in the front. They had, like, some on the sides, one on the top, a bunch of these. And then they pumped a ton of energy through them, of electricity, a ridiculous amount of electricity through them.
And so they thought that maybe it would create some kind of force field around it that would make the boat not reflect the radar.
So they did that in the bay of the river, some river, Philadelphia, I guess. A few miles down, six miles down from Philadelphia.
And so the boat disappeared to radar, but it also disappeared to the site. It completely disappeared.
It didn't even have the hole in the water from the boat. The water was just flat. It went into another dimension.
And nobody expected that to happen. And there was no radio contact.
Four hours later, the boat suddenly appeared, but there was still no radio contact.
So they aborted the boat and they found all of the sailors on there.
They were, a bunch of them were melted into the ship.
They were, arms were inside the ship and they were dying.
And then the ones that weren't inside the ship were fading in and out of visibility.
One guy, his arm was invisible.
And they had to, so they had to bandage it up.
So what happened was they were in this other dimension and they walked into their ship because they didn't know where the ship ended and where they ended.
And then when they came back into this dimension, third dimension, they were in the wrong place and they just got frozen there.
So, um, now Al Bielek, he, when this happened, they found themselves on the ship and all they saw was a purple haze.
And when they looked off into the sea and, uh, down in the water and they freaked out because their minds started to, I guess, go crazy.
Something happened with everybody's minds and they, they thought they were all going to die.
So he and this other guy jumped off the boat because they wanted to swim back to shore.
And so a couple of guys jumped off after them and they never were to see him from again.
But Al Bielek and this other guy, um, they just fell, fell, fell, fell, fell, and they never hit the water.
And they were falling for like an hour completely out of it.
Even though they're right next to each other, looking at each other, they couldn't talk to each other because they were just like this.
And when, and then they passed out and when they woke up, I think they were in 1984.
What, well, that's the movie.
I think what he says is when they woke up, they were 300 years in the future.
And they were inside of some like installation, military installation or something.
And they, and they had like people there and they were there for three months.
And then something happened and he found himself 700 years in the future and he was there for two years.
And I think he had like a government liaison, somebody who introduced him to the world there.
So somehow it was orchestrated.
Somehow they found him and this guy.
And so I guess what happened was, um, somebody decided since they jumped off, somebody rescued them from the future.
Somehow the, the ship went into the future and some, he doesn't know what happened.
So they, uh, yeah, they gave him the job as a tour guide.
And he says in the future, 700 years in the future, I think he said, there's only 500 million people on the whole planet.
Um, and, um, we have floating cities, anti-gravity cities, um, that have these anti-gravity drives that keep them floating.
And his job was a tour guide.
And he says that there wasn't really money.
There's just, you have just different jobs that are easy to train you and then you do it.
And that was his job.
Um, and then, uh, then something happened and he was sent or back to 1942.
So he came back to 1942 and he, uh, lived his life from there.
And he says that, uh, oh, the Steelers, they were involved.
They died like within a year of radiation.
And then a few of them were sent to the funny farm.

And, uh, so, but he, so he doesn't remember the, a lot of the details.
He just remembers it kind of fuzzy.
And I guess that's why he didn't give a whole lot of details about 700 years in the future.
But he said also about 700 years in the future that, um, that we grew food inside and, uh, in buildings.
And that's actually what I envisioned in my mind.
When I was in Boulder, there was this new technology called, um, aeroponics and had this thing called Aeroflow, which is really cool.
And it grows plants right out of the air.
And so, you know, I think we're going to be vegetarian in the future because killing animals is bad and it doesn't make sense economically.
But the best way to grow plants is inside so that you don't have to use pesticides.
Um, so that's what he said.
And interestingly enough, Bashar said the same thing, said that in the future we'll have floating cities and that they have floating cities.
